findings.title: position = NULL for all 65 codes across all 4 domains — undefined display order
findings.evidence: SELECT domain, code, position FROM public.ref_code WHERE domain IN ('allergen','cuisine_type','dietary_tag','menu_category') → position IS NULL for every row. The FE loader calls .order('position', { ascending: true }) for all 4 domains (object-workspace.ts lines 2578-2581). With all positions NULL, Postgres returns rows in an implementation-defined order (typically heap/insertion order), which is not guaranteed stable across vacuums. The seed INSERTs do not set position. This affects the display order of 14 allergen chips, 29 cuisine chips, 12 dietary chips, and 10 menu-category options in the §13/menu editor.
findings.recommendation: Add a position seed for each domain in seeds_data.sql (or a dedicated positions migration). Recommended ordering: allergen → EU regulation alphabetical (celery, crustaceans, dairy, eggs, fish, gluten, lupin, molluscs, mustard, nuts, peanuts, sesame, soy, sulphites); cuisine_type → local-first then alphabetical (creole, metropolitan, traditional first); dietary_tag → most-common-first (vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten_free, lactose_free, organic, local, …); menu_category → meal-flow order (entree, main, dessert, drinks, snacks, petit_dejeuner, brunch, menu_enfant, menu_groupe, menu_degustation). Use ON CONFLICT (domain, code) DO UPDATE SET position=EXCLUDED.position for idempotency.

findings.title: cuisine_type:vegan duplicates the concept expressed by dietary_tag:vegan at a different model level
findings.evidence: cuisine_type:vegan (name='Végan', en='Vegan') exists alongside dietary_tag:vegan (name='Végan', en='Vegan'). cuisine_type describes the cooking style of a restaurant (e.g. the menu as a whole); dietary_tag describes the suitability of individual menu items. Both codes are present in FE state (WorkspaceMenuModule.cuisineTypeOptions vs dietaryTagOptions). The overlap is intentional at the data model level (a restaurant can be classified as cuisine_type:vegan AND an individual menu item can carry dietary_tag:vegan), but both codes carry the same FR/EN label with no differentiating description. A future operator importing data could miscategorize menu items as cuisine_type or vice versa.
findings.recommendation: No structural change needed — the distinction is correct and the FE keeps the two separate. Harden the FR description to clarify the level: cuisine_type:vegan description → 'Établissement entièrement végane (style de cuisine global)'; dietary_tag:vegan description → 'Plat sans aucun produit d\'origine animale'. Update seeds_data.sql and redeploy the seeds block.

findings.title: zh and el translations absent — global gap, post-MVP
findings.evidence: name_i18n contains only 'en' and 'es' keys for all 14 allergen codes. zh and el keys are absent. This is consistent with the global gap across ALL ref_code domains.
findings.recommendation: Track as a single post-MVP i18n task: add zh/el translations to all food-domain codes in a single migration alongside the other ref_code domains. Do not handle per-code.

findings.title: 0 DB rows in all 4 consumer tables — classified FORWARD-LOOKING, not dead
findings.evidence: object_menu=0 rows, object_menu_item=0, object_menu_item_allergen=0, object_menu_item_cuisine_type=0, object_menu_item_dietary_tag=0 (confirmed live). The menu facet applies only to object_type=RES; there are live RES objects (ref_facet_applicability covers RES→object_menu per §46/§57) but no menus have been authored yet. All 4 domains are actively loaded by the FE menu editor (object-workspace.ts lines 2576-2581 for read, 4382-4385 for write path). The domains are also referenced as partition keys in object_taxonomy FKs and ref_code_taxonomy_closure, though 0 taxonomy rows exist for these domains.
findings.recommendation: No action. Re-evaluate at the first RES menu authoring pass: at that point, confirm all codes survive the FK constraint and that the FE correctly round-trips codes through the saver (ensureKnownCodes gate at line 4422-4424 of object-workspace.ts). Log as FORWARD-LOOKING in the deferred items tracker.

findings.title: FOR ALL write policies still present on object_menu and object_menu_item in migration_permission_write_paths.sql (source) — already superseded on live by §47 per-command migration
findings.evidence: migration_permission_write_paths.sql lines 135-159 still create FOR ALL owner_menu_item_dietary_write / owner_menu_item_allergen_write / owner_menu_item_cuisine_write. On LIVE, these are superseded by canonical_ins/upd/del families from migration_write_policy_percommand.sql (confirmed via pg_policies query: only SELECT + INS + UPD + DEL policies present, no FOR ALL). The source file is stale relative to live on these 3 tables. The §47 runbook caveat says re-run migration_write_policy_percommand.sql after a fresh rls_policies.sql apply.
findings.recommendation: No immediate action needed (live is correct). At the next major schema regen, update migration_permission_write_paths.sql to drop and replace these 3 FOR ALL policies with per-command equivalents to match the live state, or annotate with a comment that migration_write_policy_percommand.sql supersedes them. This prevents confusion during fresh-apply gate runs.
